thumbnail.alt: Image comparing costs of various climate actions, with an EV on the left at $1000 and a fuel-efficient cookstove on the right at $50

image_before_title.alt: Image comparing costs of various climate actions, with an EV on the left at $1000 and a fuel-efficient cookstove on the right at $50

short_content.text: Carbon offsets are terrible.  They are still the most effective tool we have.  If we truly want to tackle the climate crisis, we need to start treating them as such.

content.text: Carbon offsets are terrible.  They are still the most effective tool we have.  If we truly want to tackle the climate crisis, we need to start treating them as such.

When we first began researching the carbon market, we were shocked at just how cheap it was to offset a tonne of carbon, especially compared to reducing a tonne through lifestyle changes.  It’s difficult to believe that an offset can be purchased for $10 that has the same effect as quitting driving for a year.  That gap between cost and impact, it turns out, is real. The credibility failures are also real. The question is whether the second fact should cancel out the first.

The criticisms are well founded.  The largest recent meta-study by Probst et al suggests that 84% of offsets achieve nothing.  These efficacy failures have led to offsets becoming the pariah of the environmental world and are dismissed as "greenwashing", "scams", or "indulgences for the rich".  I reached out to Dr Probst for clarification, and it turns out the 16% is the average achievement ratio.  The paper can make a very different case than the critics suggest.

quotetext.text: We should honestly criticize the 84% failure rate and equally recognize the 16% success rate.

content.text: Studies consistently show offsets are far less effective than claimed - either by overstating reductions or because the action would have happened anyway.  Let’s take the most damning research at face value and compare it to other recognized forms of climate action.  We find that even randomly selecting offsets, the most CO2 reduced per dollar spent - called the Marginal Abatement Costs (MAC) - is achieved through offsets.

Let’s examine a simple example - choosing whether to install a natural gas furnace vs a cold-climate heat pump to see which makes the most climate sense.  We use the data from heatpumpcalculator.ca, a tool provided by the Canadian Climate Institute.  We exclude rebates but include the carbon tax for the same reason: neither changes the true cost, they just change who pays it.  To find the most effective climate action, we must look at the total cost to remove a tonne of CO2. If it takes $15,000 of society's money to install a heat pump, that is $15,000 that cannot be spent on other, more efficient climate solutions.
Upfront Cost
Yearly Costs
Emissions
Annualized
Cold-climate Heatpump
Gas + AC
Difference
Using average values, annualized over the system lifetime, we find that the emissions saved are 3.81 tCO2e at a cost of $348.90 per year, giving a total cost per-tonne of $91.57.

Now let’s look at carbon offsets.  For our thought experiment, we assume a completely random approach to the market, meaning we simply take the market average cost of $8.50 and divide it by the achievement ratio of 16% to get an adjusted price of $53.13 per tonne.  This results in nearly twice the CO2 avoided than the heat pump for the same cost.

A critic might point out that the emissions avoided by the heat pump are certain, while the emissions from offsets are not.  This position discounts the same research it relies on.  If we accept the research when it claims that 84% of offsets are worthless, it is not logically consistent to reject that 16% of offsets are valuable.  Furthermore, offsets are not just tree-planting, they cover a wide range of activities and many provide the same certainty as the heat-pump.  With a little due diligence, we can do much better than random chance.  As Dr Probst says: “Of course there are some projects that have achieved what they have claimed”.  Choosing a higher-quality provider like Gold Standard, sticking to sectors with more reliable outcomes, and using tools like the Carbon Credit Quality Initiative mean we are more likely to find these projects.  This often means purchasing offsets that are more expensive initially, but that result in an even better CO2-per-dollar outcome.

image.alt: Image shows 3 climate actions comparing their relative costs

caption.text: * The majority of Canadians reside in provinces with a grid emissions intensity below the CO2e cost of residential solar panels

content.text: While this may sound counterintuitive, it should be clear that it is exactly the result we would expect.  It is just incredibly unlikely that a gas furnace in Canada - which is actually fairly efficient - is going to be the most effective change in a world where people still burn kerosene for light.  The same logic applies to EV's, where the MAC can reach $1000 per tonne, and residential solar, where for most Canadians the grid is already clean enough that the abatement cost approaches infinity.  Carbon offsets are about efficiency. And whatever its flaws, a market is very good at finding it.

To be clear, we are not arguing that the best action is to install a gas furnace and use the difference to buy carbon offsets.  There are many factors that go into purchasing decisions, and this article's sole argument is about the climate impact.

We should also clarify that while they are effective, offsets are not the cheapest form of climate action.  Going vegetarian, for instance, is often cited as a 'free' climate action - it saves around 0.6 tonnes of CO₂ reduces grocery bills, and improves health. But revealed preference tells a different story.  As inflation has raised the cost of meat, the average person spends $30 more per year rather than switching. This shows that an average person clearly values their meat diet more than they value $30, and we can’t simply look at the dollar amount.

We’re also only looking at the offsets to argue their effectiveness.  How they are used is another story entirely.  The “greenwashing” and “indulgences” criticisms aren’t about offsets at all, they focus on the purchaser's behavior.  These arguments could be entirely valid even if offsets were 100% reliable, or a purchaser can work in the best faith with flawed offsets.

And finally, this isn’t to claim other forms of climate action aren’t necessary - we aren’t trying to present a false dichotomy.  However, the opportunity for offsets is enormous.  The majority of CO2e can be abated at a price below $20.  We absolutely need a pathway for these actions to happen, and right now a better one doesn’t exist.

All of this leaves us in an interesting position.  The opportunity is immense and the need is even greater.  If the climate crisis is truly a crisis, then efficiency is paramount.  Why, then, are offsets so heavily criticized?  The answer is depressingly obvious - it's because we don’t want them.  Offsets are the sole climate action that has zero personal benefit.  We focused here on the climate benefit, but a heat-pump raises your house value, vegetarianism has many health benefits, you never search for parking before leaving a bus.  Yes, offsets are flawed.  They are also, by the math, the most effective tool most of us have.  The logic is clear.  What's left is a choice.

thumbnail.alt: A Solar Home System with LED lights

image_before_title.alt: A Solar Home System with LED lights

short_content.text: Deep-dive our solar lighting offset, and how we navigate the difficult questions of additionality in for-profit climate ventures.

content.text: In this project, we helped subsidize small, portable solar home systems (SHS) sold to off-grid communities in Uganda and Kenya.  These projects are attractive because they provide a very reliable way to reduce CO2.

There was a personal attraction for me as well.  I spent my first years in a remote village without electricity.  One of my earliest memories is of my Dad marking exams by lantern light.  While it may seem romantic, the reality is that these constraints make valuable economic activity—and even basic education—incredibly difficult.

image.alt: The authors father attempts to mark exams in a room dimly lit by two kerosene lanterns, Solomon Islands, 1982

caption.text: The authors father marking exams by kerosene lantern light

content.text: However, carbon offsetting is rarely black and white, so here is our honest take on the project's strengths and its complexities.
In General

Replacing Kerosene lanterns with solar is “low-hanging fruit”. In remote areas, the most commonly used light source is kerosene.  Roughly 685 million people lack basic electricity access; collectively, they burn an estimated 2 to 10 billion liters of kerosene for light every year.  That results in well over 100 million tonnes of CO2e - about 2.5% of the total emissions of the United States.  However, while solar has plummeted in price, the cost of a solar home kit remains a difficult barrier to many, with a recent study showing a majority of households can only afford a subsidized system.
Our Project Specifically:

There are several good studies for solar lighting specifically in Uganda and Kenya, our project's location.  A randomized controlled trial in Uganda showed that introducing solar lighting - similar to the kits we sponsored - completely replaced kerosene lanterns in 92% of the households using them.

This provides a precise baseline; because the displaced fuel is known, we can calculate the CO2e reduction with high certainty. Since the new lighting method generates no emissions, the climate impact is a direct calculation rather than a vague estimate.

This is where it gets interesting. M-KOPA is a for-profit company with big-name backers like Richard Branson. Skeptics may ask: “Why do they need carbon money?” As it turns out, M-KOPA was not profitable in 2017. In fact, they only turned their first profit in 2024 after over a decade in the red. Back in 2017, their micro-financing model was still a high-risk experiment.  In fact, they were borrowing $80 million just to cover the cost of the hardware they were essentially lending to the customers. We can’t know for certain what would have happened without the carbon financing, but for a new company needing to prove its approach to creditors, it would undoubtedly have been a major positive.

Unlike many other carbon offsets, these offsets directly reduce demand for fossil fuels, the only true climate solution.  A household only has so much need for light.  Multiple studies back this up, showing only 1 in 5 households continue to use dirty lamps once they have a solar kit.  The kerosene that is displaced is not burned later due to the household storing up demand for lighting, nor does it leak to a neighbor, the reason it was burned in the first place no longer exists.

These kits include monitoring equipment to track how much they are being used.  This is important because usage is a reliable proxy for how much kerosene is being displaced.  Gold Standard also verified the project with on-site reviews of the systems, cross-checking the claims of units sold against company logbooks, inventories and purchase records.  Finally, Gold Standard interviewed 20 end users to check their experiences with the system.
This rigor leaves us very confident that these systems offset the amount of CO2e emissions claimed.
The benefits of substituting kerosene for solar are well established.  The light itself is far better, far safer, far healthier, and - over time - far cheaper.

For our project, Gold Standard also ran a survey of 400 users a few years after the launch.  The end users were happy with the product distributed under this programme, and how it had changed their lives for the better.  Many noted they no longer have to walk miles just to charge a mobile phone or buy fuel.
Conclusion

Ultimately, we are comfortable with the complexity of 'additionality' here because the results are so tangible.  To us, this is the “Better World” in action.  Our clients directly contributed to significant CO2 reductions. Even better, somewhere in Uganda and Kenya, a father is now marking exams under a steady LED bulb instead of a flickering kerosene flame.

short_content.text: Our first purchases, and first lessons

content.text: Volume: 118 Tonnes (combined)
Total Investment: $1,345 USD
Purchase Record: <not listed>

These offsets represented our first experience into the world of carbon markets. At the time, we were still navigating how offsets worked, so our primary strategy was simple: trust the experts at Gold Standard.
We opted for a “basket” purchase—a diversified collection of projects managed by Gold Standard.

While we respect and trust the expertise of Gold Standard, purchasing a basket means we don't have individual records for each specific project funded.  Since this purchase, we've moved to individual projects, giving us full transparency into the initiatives our community is supporting.

image_before_title.alt: An open hand with a glowing coin floating above it

short_content.text: No fees is easy. Earning on $0 is hard. Here's how we make something out of nothing.

content.text: Traditional investing requires money to make money.  Our goal was to create a tool that worked for as many people as possible, and to do this we instead focused on what we can do with $0.  We found a way to generate returns on an activity that all of us are doing anyway - spending money.
Want to see this in action?  Visit the demo account to see how it’s doing today.
How do the transfers happen?

While you could make all the transfers yourself, it’s practically impossible to do manually with the required consistency.  To solve this we’ve created a small app to automate this process to make sure you get the best results.  If you want to understand exactly how this works, we’ve written a full explanation here.
What happens when the market goes down?

Bad things happen.  It would be irresponsible to provide a service like this without taking that into account.  That’s why we provide protection for when our investments drop in value.  Our unique protection, the “Shock Absorber”, makes sure your balance will never be short when the bills come due.  For peace of mind, read about it here.
How much of a difference can this make?

While investing can be volatile in the short term, its real impact shows up over decades.  Our demo account has only been running for a few years, and only represents a small slice of time.  Our simulator has back-tested this system on the past 80 years of market data to show the range of what you can expect over a lifetime.

thumbnail.alt: The Caatinga biome, Brazil, showing signs of degradation

image_before_title.alt: Buenos Aires Renewable Energy Project, Brazil

short_content.text: A ceramic factory, a furnace upgrade, and the economics of keeping native forests standing.

content.text: This project involves an upgrade to a ceramic factory’s furnace in the Buenos Aires municipality of Pernambuco, Brazil, switching its primary fuel from native timber to renewable biomass (including coconut pulp and Algaroba wood). These wood types are more difficult to burn than native wood—they have lower energy density and require specialized combustion equipment—which is where the carbon financing comes in.
In General

This project involved financing a ceramic factory to upgrade its furnace to burn biomass instead of harvested forest wood.  This type of offset reduces CO2e by preventing the harvesting of long-lived trees in favor of fast-growing and quickly replenished biomass.

In biomass fuel-switching projects, the main claim is that without this project the factory would keep using non-renewable biomass from unsustainable logging.  However, these kinds of projects often have unrealistic assumptions, with a large meta-study of carbon credits finding them particularly at risk of over-crediting.  So, let's dive into this project to see how it stacks up.
Our Project Specifically:

project_documents.text: GS2290 - Buenos Aires Renewable Energy Project

content.text: To calculate the CO2e saved, you must first prove what would have been burned otherwise.  The project claims:
"The fuel utilized in the baseline scenario... was native wood from deforestation of the Caatinga biome... which is common practice in the region."

Independent studies confirm this, saying “the traditional bricks industry employs as primary fuel the native wood”, and specifically cite “the high costs of commercial non-forest energy”.  In other words, why pay more for worse fuel?
By measuring the renewable fuel consumed after the upgrade with the fuel prior, we can measure how much native forest was not consumed.  This gives a reliable indication of the emissions avoided.

One Caution: The project mentions using "wood residues" (like sawdust) if biomass runs low. Since sawdust is already a common industrial fuel, it’s “Business as Usual” and would likely mean over-crediting.  However, as a secondary fuel source, it doesn't undermine the primary win of moving away from native forest wood.

This project falls under the category of “renewable energy”, which often has very poor additionality.  Large-scale industrial operations simply can’t afford to rely on an unreliable funding source powered by goodwill, and in these cases the offsets are simply a cherry on top for investors.  However, this project is a rare case where “it only happens with climate finance”.

As an existing business, we have a clear paper record of before and after.  The raw material costs are well documented in the region, and a business generally won’t purchase more expensive equipment just to burn a more expensive fuel source.

Even more importantly, because the offsets cover the ongoing difference in fuel costs, we can be sure that each credit is responsible for its offset - without the offsets covering the difference in fuel costs, a rational business owner would simply go back to buying the cheaper forest wood.
Permanence is not a concern with this project, once the upgrades are installed they are going to stay where they are.  The ceramics produced not be re-fired later with a different fuel source.

However, there is a strong risk of leakage.  The demand for energy is increasing and it’s possible that another factory will simply buy more native wood.  An audit of the project however found that the renewable biomass suppliers had switched their businesses from harvesting forest wood specifically because of this project.  Further, the project has influenced other factories to start purchasing renewable biomasses in an example of “good” leakage.
This doesn't resolve the issue entirely, but it highlights how leakage is a systemic challenge. The answer is broader adoption of this model, not abandoning an offset that demonstrably works.

A key reason for using Gold Standard is their robust verification.  When issuing the credits, an auditor reviewed the fuel invoices and delivery records to verify it was renewable biomass.  The auditor also followed up with an on-site inspection to review the hardware usage and interviewed both the employees and a biomass supplier.

The Caatinga biome is one of the world's 37 major wilderness areas, one of the most biodiverse semi-arid regions in the world, and the only exclusively Brazilian biome.  It’s also one of the most heavily degraded, with desertification threatening 15% of its area.  There is very little formal protection for the ecology, and its local population lives in extreme poverty.

Protecting the environment of this unique area is difficult under these circumstances, but offsets such as these can set in motion virtuous cycles.  When sustainable biomass becomes the economically rational fuel source, it creates demand for the agricultural activity that produces it — giving local communities a viable alternative to deforestation, rather than a moral argument against it.  The audit verified this, as biomass suppliers had started entirely new formal businesses specifically because of this project, improving livelihoods beyond the direct project participants.
Conclusion

This offset does what it claims: a verified furnace upgrade, continued use of renewable fuels, and a funding mechanism that holds the economics honest.  However, deforestation continues in the region, and critics may note that the demand we helped suppress may have simply moved elsewhere.  We don’t dispute that.  The goal isn’t to declare victory, it’s to prove that the shift is possible and keep moving towards it.  These offsets show this works, and we intend to keep taking these steps until they are applied at the necessary scale.

short_content.text: Our first offset deep-dives, validating our approach

content.text: Volume: 47 Tonnes
Total Investment: $517 USD
Purchase Record: GSM2486

This is the first quarter for which we have project-level documentation.  We’ve used this documentation to publish project-level reviews, increasing both our transparency and understanding.  We continued investing in a “basket” approach, believing that diversification would give us the best results overall.

In this purchase we supported the following 5 projects:
 - Solar Lighting
 - Ceramic Firing
 - Landfill Gas Extraction (review coming soon)
 - Improved Cookstoves (2 projects in Malawi & Peru - review coming soon)

We consider this an excellent example of the outcomes TheCoin is working to enable.  The reviews show the credits represent real reductions and meaningful co-benefits for local communities.  The cost of these offsets is far below the relative costs of many other forms of climate advocacy, validating our approach.

thumbnail.alt: TheCoin logo and Gold Standard Logo paired with a heart

image_before_title.alt: TheCoin logo and Gold Standard Logo paired with a heart

short_content.text: Carbon Credits suffer a crisis of confidence.  Here we discuss the first step we chose in trying to ensure the action we take is meaningful and verifiable.

content.text: Ensuring Our Climate Action is Real
Here at TheCoin, our goal is to make climate action selfish. We thought the “selfish” part—creating a financial win for our users—would be the hardest part.
As it turned out, that wasn’t the biggest challenge. The real difficulty is ensuring the “climate action” part actually happens.

When we first entered this field, we were overwhelmed. You can buy carbon credits from hundreds of different vendors at wildly different prices. Because there is very little global regulation on how this market operates, a lot of “junk” has entered the market. This complexity is exactly why carbon credits get so much flak: it is far too easy to accidentally buy credits that don't achieve anything.
Simplifying the Field
To cut through the noise, we made a core decision: we only purchase offsets from Gold Standard.

While there is plenty of debate regarding the flaws in the wider carbon market, Gold Standard is globally recognized as the highest-quality provider. It is frequently described as the "go-to quality label" because of its rigorous requirements and reputation for integrity.
What is Gold Standard?
Gold Standard acts as both a certification standard for projects and a registry to track them.

Think of them as the essential middleman. There are countless ways to reduce fossil fuel use, but many of them don't happen simply because they aren't profitable on their own. Gold Standard verifies that a project does exactly what it claims to do, then connects it with funders (like us) who can provide the financing to make it a reality.
Why Gold Standard is Different
We chose Gold Standard for four key reasons:

Founded by the World Wildlife Fund (WWF): We believe that motivations matter. Gold Standard was established in 2003 by the WWF and other international NGOs. The WWF has spent decades on the difficult, often thankless task of protecting nature and its resources. While a famous name is no guarantee of success, it ensures that Gold Standard’s DNA is rooted in conservation rather than corporate “check-the-box” compliance. This is why over 80 NGOs have endorsed Gold Standard, something no other carbon registry can claim.

Radical Transparency: Every time we purchase a credit, we can access the project's complete documentation. We know who is running the project, how they calculate their CO2 savings, and exactly why they need our financing. This transparency is key for us to verify the credits are reliable and beneficial to everyone involved.

Third-Party Validation: Gold Standard requires independent auditors to verify a project’s goals and outcomes. This isn't just a paperwork check; it often includes physical site visits to ensure the project is delivering results year after year.

A Focus on "Co-Benefits": For us, it isn't enough to just count carbon. Each project must demonstrate how it helps the local community. For example, you could spend $350 to capture a ton of CO2 from the air, or you could spend that same $350 to provide a solar power system for a remote community. While both help the climate, the solar system provides light, safety, and opportunity for real people. To us, that is an investment worth making on its own merits.
Quality Over Quantity

We aren't looking for a cheap way to check a box. We choose Gold Standard because they provide the transparency and integrity needed to prove that when you use our platform, you aren’t just "cancelling out" your impact—you are actively participating in building that "better world," regardless of the politics or the math.

short_content.text: Climate Portfolio Offset procured by TheCoin

content.text: For this offset, we chose to do something different. Instead of going for an offset that targets one area of impact in emission reduction, we went for a combination of projects that collectively would reduce greenhouse emissions. The offset comprises energy efficiency projects, solar, wind, and biogas power, agricultural reforestation, and clean water access. So, why a combination of projects and not one? You may ask. Well! After internal deliberations, we gathered that there is no one silver bullet when dealing with climate change.

On the contrary, to effectively reduce greenhouse emissions, a combination of efforts is required. What better way to express this than to invest in an offset that combines multiple projects with a broader objective of reducing greenhouse gasses? You see our thinking. At the heart of climate change issues is a problem that is relatively easy to miss: energy consumption. Humans will always use energy. That is not even debatable. However, our energy use must be sustainable. How?

For one, we could start being more efficient in our energy use. By minimizing energy waste, our overall energy consumption reduces, effectively reducing our carbon footprint. I hope you now understand why energy efficiency is one of the projects under this offset. Secondly, we ought to move to more sustainable sources of energy. Here, renewable energy sources such as solar, wind, and biogas power are the way to go. Solar power will always exist and has minimal damage to the environment. On the other hand, wind power is not only renewable but accessible in most Western Countries, which are the largest emitters of greenhouse gasses. As for biogas, this energy source is renewable and uses waste, making it both efficient and effective in emission reduction.

Away from reducing emissions, we also need to look at the areas most impacted by climate change and mitigate some of the adverse effects. These areas include agricultural and water systems. Emissions have disrupted global weather patterns, directly impacting agriculture, which relies on the same. Similarly, due to climate change, our water systems have been adversely affected by unpredictable floods and an unhealthy water cycle. By mitigating the adverse effects of climate change in these areas, while simultaneously focusing on energy efficiency and renewable energy, we will have dealt a major blow to climate change, hence, our inclusion of all these projects in this offset.
Do you think procuring this offset was a wise decision or a poor one? Visit our contact page and share your response. We will keep your feedback in mind when procuring the next offset.

short_content.text: Learn how even better cooking facilities is good for everyone

content.text: This project is our most current carbon offset. We settled for it due to its combined climate change and social impacts. Implemented in Uganda, Kenya, and Tanzania, the project focuses on biogas, a renewable form of energy. Biogas is produced by the decomposition of animal and plant waste in an oxygen-starved environment and is used as an alternative to fuel and natural gas. The anaerobic digestion that occurs in oxygen-deficient environments breaks down the waste into methane and carbon dioxide. I know! You are probably wondering how biogas reduces climate change, yet it releases carbon dioxide and methane, which are both greenhouse gasses, into the atmosphere.

Well! Here’s your brief answer. First of all, it is true that both methane and carbon dioxide are greenhouse gasses and contribute to global warming. However, please note that plants, which are responsible for fixing carbon into the soil are the primary source of biogas. So, any emissions from the plant waste are essentially carbon neutral. That is why plants and trees are not considered sources of emissions despite releasing carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. Their net carbon emission contribution is zero since they fix and release carbon into the atmosphere in equal measure. This point is the primary difference between biogas and fossil fuels. While biogas comes from plants that remove carbon dioxide and methane from the atmosphere, fossil fuels do not remove emissions at all. They only contribute.

You still don’t get it? Okay. Let’s try another angle. You see, biogas is a renewable source of energy. Like wind and solar power, biogas can be continuously generated from plant and animal waste. So long as plants and animals exist, they will always release waste, which can be digested into biogas. This is why they are considered a renewable source of energy. Renewable energy sources have a net positive effect on emission reduction compared to nonrenewable sources such as fossil fuels. I hope you now get the point.  If you don’t, you will get it by the time you are done reading this article.

Now, back to our offset, the countries hosting this project cut down trees and use them to make charcoal. Notice, they do not use plant waste. Instead, they cut down living trees, which would otherwise remove carbon from the atmosphere. Unlike in the case of biogas, which relies on natural plant or animal waste, effectively being carbon neutral, cutting down trees removes their carbon elimination potential. Worse off, instead of letting the cut down trees decompose into natural waste, they burn them into charcoal, a process that releases carbon monoxide into the atmosphere.

Even more problematic, when the charcoal is burned as a fuel, it releases more carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. So, we have three problems here. First, trees that would otherwise remove carbon dioxide from the atmosphere have been cut down, leading to more carbon in the environment. Secondly, the cut-down trees are converted to charcoal, a process that releases even more carbon dioxide into the atmosphere. Thirdly, charcoal as a source of fuel releases more greenhouse gasses. Compare that to using biogas, which relies on natural waste only, making it carbon neutral. Biogas is also renewable as plant wastes occur naturally. See why we invested in this offset. By allowing charcoal users in these countries to use biogas instead of charcoal, we have prevented huge volumes of carbon dioxide from entering the atmosphere.
Do you think procuring this offset was a wise decision or a poor one? Visit our contact page and share your response. We will keep your feedback in mind when procuring the next offset.

short_content.text: Think investing is only for the wealthy?  Think again - here is a simple technique to generate serious money without saving a single penny!

content.text: When people hear of investing, a lot of us switch off.  Investing happens in our RRSP account or to people who have so much money they don't need it all.  It doesn't need to be like that.  We designed TheCoin for everyone.  We won't solve climate change if only the wealthy can afford the solution.

Here, we show how even someone with no savings can safely and efficiently boost their income with TheCoin.  This technique uses only the money you are spending.  You will offset a large part of your carbon footprint, but more importantly, you can generate hundreds of thousands of dollars over your lifetime.

All you need:
1) A money-back visa card.
2) An account with TheCoin.

This technique exploits the interest-free grace period most visa cards offer to create a balance with TheCoin.  When you purchase things with your Visa, you receive a short amount of time to pay back the purchase price without being charged interest.  Most cards have a billing cycle of 4 weeks.  The purchases are tallied up at the end of the billing cycle to calculate the amount due.  You then have three weeks to pay the amount owing in full.  If you do this, then no interest is charged on the card.

The combined billing cycle and due date mean you have up to 7 weeks where your purchases do not charge any interest.  At the same time, you've been earning the money needed to pay off the credit card.  This cash usually is sitting in your bank, not doing anything useful.  You could keep this money in TheCoin and watch it grow instead.

Seven weeks is not a long time, certainly not long enough to make any significant difference to your life.  The full power of this technique comes from the fact that you will repeat this process every four weeks for the rest of your life.  You don't invest for seven weeks - this small amount can remain invested for decades to come.  A small amount invested over a long time -can- change your life.  To demonstrate how, let's walk through this week by week, year by year.

Amy is 23 years old.  She's not a high earner or a big spender.  Amy has no savings and only earns enough each week to cover the $100 she spends on groceries and the odd night out.  She has just learned of TheCoin and decided it's time to make her money work for her.  She absolutely cannot afford to lose any money, so she creates her account with the maximum ShockAbsorber protection of 50%.  This level of protection will protect her from even once-in-a-lifetime recessions by topping up her account if the stock market drops.  After receiving her cash-back visa, she is ready to begin.

The first week, Amy puts her $100 spending on her visa card.  At the same time, she deposits her $100 earnings into TheCoin.  Her net balance is still $0 - she owes Visa $100 and has $100 in her account.  Amy spends another $100 the following week and deposits $100 in her coin account.  Her net balance is still $0; she now owes Visa $200 and has $200 with TheCoin.  Week 3 and 4 repeat the pattern, and at the end of week 4, Visa calculates her bill of $400, due in 3 weeks.  However, instead of immediately paying, Amy uses TheCoins auto-pay feature to pay on the last date possible.

Paying late achieves nothing in a regular bank account, but it makes a significant difference when your balance grows itself.  For Amy, even though her net balance continues to be $0, on week five her coin account grows to $500, and by the end of week seven it has grown to $700.    At the end of week seven, her visa bill is automatically paid, and she starts week 8 with $300 in her account.  At the end of week 8, Visa calculates her next bill of $400, with three weeks to pay it off.  Amy continues earning and spending until the due date.  On week 11, Amy again has $700 in the bank before paying the $400.  The cycle repeats.

Following this technique, Amys' balance with TheCoin stays between $300 on her billing date and $700 on her due date.  Her average balance is $500, and she is earning and spending $5200 each year.  A basic coin account will grow around 9% on an average year.  However, Amys' ShockAbsorber uses the first 6% of that profit to protect her from market crashes.  This means her actual gains will be a little lower; 9% - 6% is 3%, or around $15.  Her cash-back visa does quite a bit better, depositing $52 into her account at the end of her first year for a total extra savings of $67.

While it seems that TheCoin account isn't doing much to help, it gets more interesting in the following years.  Amy immediately puts her $52 into her coin account (her $15 is already there) and continues life as usual.  This additional $67 is savings, so she doesn't add it to her ShockAbsorber.  Even if the market drops, she isn't spending it anyway, so she can let it grow as fast as possible.  Now, her account gains $15 from the base $500 and another $6 from her savings for a total of $21.  With another $52 from her cash-back card, she now has an additional $140 in savings.

From these humble beginnings, Amy is on a path to genuine wealth.  After three years, her coin account grows an additional $15 + $13 for total savings of $220.  After four years, her account grows $15 + $20 for total savings of $306.  By the time Amy reaches 30 years old, she has an additional $616 in savings, and her coin account is growing faster than the cash-back card.

As she continues through life, Amys' account keeps growing.  Assuming she retires at 65, Amy will have an extra $30000, but it doesn't stop there.    Amys' account will continue to grow throughout retirement as she earns and spends to give lifetime earnings of well over $100K.
All of this, without saving a single penny.  Thanks, Visa!

There is another aspect to this story, and it is far more critical.  In Amys' first year with TheCoin, she offset 1.7 tonnes of CO2.  1.7 tonnes is more CO2 removed than comes from your car.  By 30, Amy removed 4.5 tonnes of CO2 from the atmosphere each year.  After 33 years, she is officially carbon-negative.  Over her lifespan, she removed far more carbon from the atmosphere than she put in.

Little choices can have enormous consequences.  Amy didn't do anything out of the ordinary.  She didn't save money or change her lifestyle.  Her only difference was a small choice made at a young age; She started using TheCoin as her spending account.  Apart from that one moment, everything else was the same.  However, that small moment resulted in a seismic shift in her life.  She reached levels of wealth she otherwise could never attain.  She did it with almost zero risk.  And, she did her part for the climate crisis, a struggle we all are part of.

1) Of course, this story has some very unrealistic scenarios.  For example, Amy would almost certainly spend some of her savings before retirement.  It's also doubtful she could go her whole life on such meagre earnings - $100 weekly is challenging to survive on today, but inflation will make it impossible in a few short years.  The article is intended to demonstrate the difference TheCoin would make assuming all other aspects of life are identical, rather than be a full simulation.

2) This article also uses a constant rate of 9% return.  In the real world, things are much messier, but that can be quite a good thing.  You can see what would happen to Amy if she tried this in the real world with our simulator - including her best & worst-case scenarios.  We encourage you to play around with the simulator to see how a scenario more similar to your own plays out

3) It's important to note that Amy has not paid any interest through this entire period.  The moment you slip up, Visa will gleefully slam you with interest rates so high they negate this whole technique.  We aren't in the business of helping Visa make money, so our app automates the process of paying the balance due at exactly the right time.

short_content.text: Turkey Wind Project Carbon Offset procured by TheCoin

content.text: For this offset, we went for a wind power project in Turkey. Our choice of this offset is informed by its tangible contribution to different facets of climate change, including transitioning from fossils to renewable energy sources and providing a sustainable source of emission avoidance. More importantly, the project is aligned to four Sustainable Development Goals that serve as concrete proof of its impact on the environment. In that regard, the project contributes to SDG 13 focused on climate action, SDG 7 on affordable and clean energy, SDG 8 on decent work and economic growth, and SDG 6 on clean water and sanitation. On SDG 13, the project stopped 131,241 tons of carbon dioxide, 36.3 tons of carbon monoxide, and 7.7 tons of Non-methane Volatile Organic Compounds (NVOC) from being released into the atmosphere. As for SDG 7 and 8, the project prevented the importation of 23,294 cubic meters of natural gas into Turkey and ensured social contribution by employing locals from the project area. Safety and Health training conducted in the project contributed to SDG 8. Lastly, on SDG 6, the project ensured that 4,912 cubic meters of wastewater discharge were avoided.

Sustainable Development Goals aside, you may wonder how wind power projects contribute to combating climate change. Well! Here’s a basic introduction. Wind energy reduces pressure on other nonrenewable energy sources such as fossil fuels. The reduced pressure eventually translates to reduced consumption of these harmful energy sources. Remember, nonrenewable energy sources are the most significant contributors to greenhouse emissions. Thus, by reducing their consumption, we will have effectively reduced their negative impact on climate change. This reduction allows for the replenishment of natural resources such as plants and animals that would otherwise be adversely affected by the heavy presence of greenhouse emissions in the atmosphere.

Moreover, wind energy projects stimulate economic growth in the wind energy sector, creating a multiplier effect throughout the economy centered around energy sources that are not harmful to the climate. The social impacts of wind projects such as creating local employment and promoting environmentally friendly practices are also worth noting. After all, the main objective of combating climate change is to improve the quality of human life by promoting sustainable co-existence between people and our environment.
Do you think procuring this offset was a wise decision or a poor one? Visit our contact page and share your response. We will keep your feedback in mind when procuring the next offset.

short_content.text: Vous pensez que l'investissement est réservé aux riches ? Détrompez-vous - voici une technique simple pour générer de l'argent  sans mettre de côté un seul centime !

content.text: Lorsque les gens entendent parler d'investissement, beaucoup d'entre nous se ferment. L'investissement se fait dans notre compte REER ou pour des personnes qui ont plus d'argent qu'elles n'en ont besoin. C'est la raison pour laquelle nous avons conçu TheCoin pour tout le monde. Nous ne résoudrons pas le changement climatique si seuls les riches peuvent se permettre la solution.

Nous vous montrerons comment, même une personne sans épargne, peut augmenter ses revenus en toute sécurité et efficacement avec TheCoin. Cette technique utilise seulement l'argent que vous dépensez. Vous compenserez une grande partie de votre empreinte carbone, mais plus important encore, vous pourrez générer des centaines de milliers de dollars au cours de votre vie.

Tout ce dont vous avez besoin :
1) Une cartes Visa assorties d’une remise en argent.
2) Un compte TheCoin.

Cette technique exploite la période de grâce sans intérêt offerte par la plupart des cartes Visa pour créer un solde avec TheCoin. Lorsque vous faites des achats avec votre Visa, vous bénéficiez d'un court laps de temps pour rembourser le prix d'achat sans intérêts. La plupart des cartes ont un cycle de facturation de 4 semaines. Les achats sont comptés à la fin du cycle de facturation pour calculer le montant dû. Vous disposez alors de trois semaines pour régler la totalité de la somme due. Si vous faites cela, aucun intérêt n'est facturé sur la carte.

Le cycle de facturation combiné et la date d'échéance signifient que vous avez jusqu'à 7 semaines pendant lesquelles vos achats ne génèrent aucun intérêt. En même temps, vous avez gagné l'argent nécessaire pour rembourser la carte de crédit. Cet argent est généralement déposé dans votre compte bancaire et ne fait rien d'utile. Vous pouvez conserver cet argent dans votre compte TheCoin et le regarder fructifier à la place.

Sept semaines, ce n'est pas long, certainement pas assez long pour faire une différence significative dans votre vie. La puissance de cette technique vient du fait que vous répéterez ce processus toutes les quatre semaines pour le reste de votre vie. Vous n'investissez pas pendant sept semaines - ce petit montant peut rester investi pendant des décennies. Un petit montant investi sur une longue période -peut- changer votre vie. Pour démontrer comment, utilisons un exemple.

Amy a 23 ans. Elle n'est pas une personne qui gagne beaucoup d'argent ou qui dépense beaucoup. Amy n'a pas d'économies et ne gagne que suffisamment chaque semaine pour couvrir les 100 $ qu'elle dépense en épicerie et en sorties nocturnes. Elle vient d'apprendre l'existence de TheCoin et a décidé qu'il était temps de faire travailler son argent pour elle. Elle ne peut absolument pas se permettre de perdre de l'argent, alors elle crée son compte avec la protection ShockAbsorber maximale de 50%. Ce niveau de protection la protégera des récessions, même uniques, en alimentant son compte en cas de baisse du marché boursier. Après avoir reçu sa visa remise en argent, elle est prête à commencer.

La première semaine, Amy met ses 100 $ de dépenses sur sa carte Visa. En même temps, elle dépose ses 100 $ de gains dans TheCoin. Son solde net est toujours de 0 $ - elle doit 100 $ à Visa et a 100 $ sur son compte. Amy dépense encore 100 $ la semaine suivante et dépose 100 $ sur son compte de pièces. Son solde net est toujours de 0 $; elle doit maintenant 200 $ à Visa et a 200 $ avec TheCoin. Les semaines 3 et 4 répètent le schéma, et à la fin de la semaine 4, Visa calcule sa facture de 400 $, due dans 3 semaines. Cependant, au lieu de payer immédiatement, Amy utilise la fonction de paiement automatique TheCoin pour payer le dernier jour possible.

Payer en retard n'apporte rien dans un compte bancaire ordinaire, mais cela fait une différence significative lorsque votre solde augmente lui-même. Pour Amy, même si son solde net continue d'être de 0 $, la cinquième semaine, son compte TheCoin passe à 500 $, et à la fin de la semaine sept, il est passé à 700 $. À la fin de la semaine sept, sa facture Visa est automatiquement payée et elle commence la semaine 8 avec 300 $ sur son compte. À la fin de la semaine 8, Visa calcule sa prochaine facture de 400 $, avec trois semaines pour la payer. Amy continue de gagner et de dépenser jusqu'à la date d'échéance. À la semaine 11, Amy a de nouveau 700 $ en banque avant de payer les 400 $. Le cycle se répète.

Grâce à cette technique, le solde d'Amy avec TheCoin reste entre 300 $ à sa date de facturation et 700 $ à sa date d'échéance. Son solde moyen est de 500 $, et elle gagne et dépense 5 200 $ chaque année. Un compte TheCoin de base augmentera, en moyenne, d'environ 9 % sur une année. Cependant, le ShockAbsorber d'Amy utilise les premiers 6 % de ce bénéfice pour la protéger des krachs boursiers. Cela signifie que ses gains réels seront un peu inférieurs ; 9 % - 6 % est de 3 %, soit environ 15 $. Sa Visa remise en argent fait un peu mieux, déposant 52 $ sur son compte à la fin de sa première année pour une économie supplémentaire totale de 67 $.

Bien qu'il semble que le compte TheCoin ne fasse pas grand-chose pour aider, il devient plus intéressant dans les années suivantes. Amy met immédiatement ses 52 $ sur son compte TheCoin (ses 15 $ sont déjà là) et continue sa vie comme d'habitude. Ces 67 $ supplémentaires représentent des économies, elle ne les ajoute donc pas à son ShockAbsorber. Même si le marché baisse, elle ne le dépense pas de toute façon, alors elle peut le laisser croître. Maintenant, son compte gagne 15 $ sur les 500 $ de base et 6 $ supplémentaires sur ses économies pour un total de 21 $. Avec 52 $ supplémentaires de sa carte de remise en argent, elle a maintenant 140 $ d'économies supplémentaires.

Même avec ces humbles débuts, Amy est sur la voie d'une véritable richesse. Après trois ans, son compte TheCoin augmente de 15 $ + 13 $ supplémentaires pour une économie totale de 220 $. Après quatre ans, son compte augmente de 15 $ + 20 $ pour une épargne totale de 306 $. Au moment où Amy atteint l'âge de 30 ans, elle dispose de 616 $ d'économies supplémentaires et son compte de pièces augmente plus rapidement que la carte de remboursement.

Au fur et à mesure qu'elle continue sa vie, le compte d'Amy ne cesse de croître. En supposant qu'elle prenne sa retraite à 65 ans, Amy aura 30 000 $ supplémentaires, mais cela ne s'arrête pas là. Le compte d'Amy continuera de croître tout au long de sa retraite à mesure qu'elle gagne et dépense pour générer des revenus à vie de plus de 100 000 $.
Tout cela, sans changer son style de vie. Merci Visa !

Il y a un autre aspect à cette histoire, et il est beaucoup plus critique. Au cours de la première année d'Amy avec TheCoin, elle a aussi compensé 1,7 tonne de CO2. 1,7 tonne, c'est plus de CO2 éliminé que ce qui provient de votre voiture. À 30 ans, Amy a éliminé 4,5 tonnes de CO2 de l'atmosphère chaque année. Après 33 ans, elle est officiellement négative en carbone. Au cours de sa vie, elle a retiré beaucoup plus de carbone de l'atmosphère qu'elle n'en a mis.

De petits décisions peuvent avoir des conséquences énormes. Amy n'a rien fait d'extraordinaire. Elle n'a pas économisé d'argent ni changé son mode de vie. Sa seule différence était un petit choix fait à un jeune âge. Elle a commencé à utiliser TheCoin comme compte de dépenses. A part ça, tout est pareil. Cependant, ce petit moment a entraîné un changement sismique dans sa vie. Elle a atteint des niveaux de richesse qu'elle n'aurait jamais pu atteindre autrement. Elle l'a fait avec un risque quasi nul. Et, elle a fait sa part pour la crise climatique, une lutte dont nous faisons tous partie.

1) Bien sûr, ce scénario est irréalistes. Par exemple, Amy dépenserait presque certainement une partie de ses économies avant la retraite. Il est également improbable qu'elle puisse vivre toute sa vie avec 100 $ par semaine, l'inflation rendra cela impossible dans quelques années. L'exemple est là pour montrer la différence qu'un compte TheCoin pourrai faire.

2) Cet article utilise également un taux constant de rendement de 9 %. Dans le monde réel, les choses sont beaucoup plus compliquées, mais cela peut être une bonne chose. Vous pouvez voir ce qui arriverait à Amy si elle essayait cela dans le monde réel avec notre simulateur - y compris ses meilleurs et pires scénarios. Nous vous encourageons à jouer avec le simulateur pour voir comment se déroulerai un scénario plus similaire au vôtre.

3) Il est important de noter qu'Amy n'a payé aucun intérêt pendant toute cette période. Au moment où vous vous trompez, Visa vous claque joyeusement avec des taux d'intérêt si élevés qu'ils annulent toute cette technique. Nous ne sommes pas là pour aider Visa à gagner de l'argent, donc notre application automatise le processus de paiement du solde dû exactement au bon moment.

short_content.text: Ouis Ouis, comment c'est possible gagner les plus gros possible?

content.text: Non non, ca faire rein.  There is one trick to investing that outshines all others.  The one "hack" that isn't a hack at all, but pretty much guaranteed to work.  The one thing everyone says you should do, but nobody does.
It is simple.  Get Started!

For a simple example, which one do you think is better?
Saving $100 each month, starting at 20?
Saving $500 each month, starting at 35?

Yup, you guessed it, in todays rat-race it's actually the turtles who come out on top.  You don't need to make a huge income to have a huge balance, you just need to know a few tricks and you'll smash the best wall-street performance metrics.
Time is the biggest multiplier possible.

short_content.text: How can you get the best possible results on your investments?  It's simple - just wait!  Time is the greatest advantage possible in investing - and we'll show you how you can maximize this advantage.

It is simple.  Get Started!

For a simple example, which one do you think is better?
Saving $100 each month, starting at 20?
Saving $500 each month, starting at 35?

Yup, you guessed it, in todays rat-race it's actually the turtles who come out on top.  You don't need to make a huge income to have a huge balance, you just need to know a few tricks and you'll smash the best wall-street performance metrics.
